title: Module 1: Citation Management
text:
After completing this module, you will be able to:

Set up and use a citation management tool (Zotero or Paperpile)
Organize and sync your reference library
Generate citations and bibliographies in any citation style
Understand what a Biosketch is and how to create one for grant applications

title: Module 1: Citation Management
text:
Overview
This module introduces citation management and two free tools: Zotero and Paperpile. It also covers the Biosketch, a document required for grant applications. Go through the summary texts on this page and review the presentations, recordings, guides, and other resources in the Resources Section to learn more.

✅ Complete the assignment at the end of this module and the associated knowledge check quiz on Canvas to earn credit toward your certificate!

title: Module 1: Citation Management
text:
Why Citation Management Matters
Manually tracking hundreds of references is error-prone and time-consuming. Citation management software lets you:

Capture references from databases, websites, and PDFs in one click
Auto-generate citations and bibliographies in any style (APA, MLA, Chicago, etc.)
Sync your library across devices and collaborate with team members
Attach and annotate PDFs alongside your references

title: Module 1: Citation Management
text:
Zotero vs. Paperpile

Choosing the Right Tool
Both tools are free to start and cover similar core workflow. Review the presentation slides and recordings in the Resources Section to learn more.



Feature
Zotero
Paperpile




Price
Free (300 MB storage)
UCR Subscription using UCR email address


Best for
Word, LibreOffice, standalone use
Google Docs users


Browser extension
✅ All browsers
✅ Google Chrome


Collaboration
✅ Group libraries
✅ Shared folders


Mobile app
✅
✅


PDF annotation
✅ Built-in
✅ Built-in





Zotero

Download at zotero.org — freemium (subscription comes with more storage but the features are the same), open source
Install the Zotero Connector browser extension to save references in one click
Integrates with Microsoft Word, LibreOffice, and Google Docs
Sync your library across devices with a free Zotero account



Paperpile

UCR Subscription using UCR email address
Works entirely in the Google Docs environment — no separate app needed
Install the browser extension and Google Docs add-on
Best choice if your entire workflow lives in Google Drive

title: Module 1: Citation Management
text:
Biosketches for Grant Applications
A biosketch is a short professional biography required by federal funders (NIH, NSF) as part of grant applications. It summarizes your education, positions, contributions to science, and research support.

NIH and NSF each have slightly different biosketch formats
The UCR Library guide walks you through each section step by step
Creating your biosketch early before grant deadlines saves significant time

title: Module 2: Scholarly Communication
text:
After completing this module, you will be able to:

Create and maintain an ORCID iD
Understand persistent identifiers (PIDs) and their role in research visibility
Explain the difference between gold, green, and diamond open access
Describe how eScholarship and the UC Open Access Policy apply to you
Take initial steps to build your online academic profile

title: Module 2: Scholarly Communication
text:
Overview
This module introduces online academic profiles, open access publishing, and the tools that make your scholarship visible and discoverable. In addition to this summary webpage, review the presentation slides, recordings, and other resources in the Resources Section to learn more. ORCID is the one thing you must remember after taking this module!

✅ Complete the assignment at the end of this module and the associated knowledge check quiz on Canvas to earn credit toward your certificate!

title: Module 2: Scholarly Communication
text:
ORCID: Your Persistent Research Identity
ORCID (Open Researcher and Contributor ID) is a free, unique digital identifier that distinguishes you from every other researcher, even those with the same name.

Why ORCID Matters

Ensures your publications are correctly attributed to you regardless of name changes or institutional affiliations
Required by many funders (NIH, NSF) and journals for submission
Connects to other research systems (Scopus, Web of Science, funding databases) automatically
Saves time to document your research contributions
Provides “unconventional” fine-grained ways of documenting your research contributions, such as peer review experiences and services.



Getting Started with ORCID

Register at orcid.org. It’s free and takes less than 5 minutes
Add your works — import from PubMed, CrossRef, or manually
Make your ORCID record public to maximize visibility
Add your ORCID iD to your email signature, CV, and manuscript submissions
Learn more about how to get started with ORCID and detailed instructions on ORCID Here and Here

title: Module 2: Scholarly Communication
text:
Open Access Publishing
Open access (OA) means making your research freely available online to anyone, anywhere, without a paywall. You might have heard about other terms starting with “open”, such as open data, open source, open educational resources (OER). But in this section, we focus on open access publishing.

Three Common Types of Open Access



Types of Open Access: Gold, Green, and Diamond





Type
Description
Cost to Author




Gold OA
Published in a fully open access journal
Article Processing Charge (APC) — Use the JOLT journal look up tool to see if UC Open Access Agreement can cover the charges


Green OA
Deposit a preprint or accepted manuscript in a repository, such as eScholarship (UC’s institutional repository)
Free


Diamond OA
Published open access with no cost to author or reader
Free

title: Module 2: Scholarly Communication
text:
eScholarship & the UC System
eScholarship is the UC System’s open access publishing platform and institutional repository. It gives departments, research units, and individual scholars direct control over disseminating their scholarship.

UCR’s eScholarship instance: escholarship.org/uc/ucr
As a UCR graduate student, you can deposit published articles (most likely but consult with UCR eScholarship liaison to learn more: Jing Han · jingh@ucr.edu), presentations, recordings, and other academic work
Your ProQuest thesis/dissertation submissions are deposited into eScholarship automatically
Questions? Contact UCR eScholarship liaison: Jing Han · jingh@ucr.edu

title: Module 2: Scholarly Communication
text:
UC Open Access Policy
The UC Open Access Policy allows UC employees (including graduate students paid by the UC system) to:

Retain copyright to their work
Grant UC a non-exclusive license to distribute and display their work
Deposit articles in an open access repository or publish in an OA journal




UC Open Access Policy Coverage


More information: UC Open Access Policy · Questions: Jing Han · jingh@ucr.edu

title: Module 2: Scholarly Communication
text:
Frequently Asked Questions






NoteWhat is eScholarship and how is it related to me as a UCR graduate student?





eScholarship is the UC System’s Open Access Publishing Platform and Institutional Repository. It provides scholarly publishing and repository services that enable departments, research units, publishing programs, and individual scholars associated with the University of California to have direct control over the creation and dissemination of the full range of their scholarship.
Each UC campus has its own instance. You can explore UCR’s eScholarship instance HERE. As a UCR graduate student, you can deposit a copy of your academic work into eScholarship. Your ProQuest thesis and dissertation submissions will be deposited automatically.









NoteDoes the UC Open Access Policy apply to me as a graduate student?





If you are a graduate student paid by the UC system, then this policy applies to you. You may opt out of the policy for any article for any reason. Contact Jing Han (jingh@ucr.edu) if you have questions.









NoteShould my ORCID record only include peer-reviewed articles?





No. Your ORCID record should include all types of scholarly outputs — peer-reviewed articles, preprints, conference presentations, datasets, software, and more. The more complete your record, the better it reflects your full research contribution.

title: Module 7 (Capstone): Research Plan
text:
After completing this module, you will be able to:

Explain what a research plan is and why every researcher needs one
Identify the key components of a graduate research plan
Draft a comprehensive research plan that integrates competencies from all modules
Submit your capstone to earn the Graduate Research Core Competencies Certificate

title: Module 7 (Capstone): Research Plan
text:
What Is a Research Plan?
A research plan is a structured document that maps out your research goals, methods, and resources. It serves multiple purposes:

For you: Clarifies your thinking, identifies gaps, and keeps your project on track
For your committee: Shows your readiness to conduct independent research
For grant applications: Many funders require a research plan or project narrative
For your career: Demonstrates scholarly organization and planning skills

title: Module 7 (Capstone): Research Plan
section: Key Components of a Graduate Research Plan
text:
Key Components of a Graduate Research Plan
A strong research plan typically includes:

1. Research Overview

Your research question or thesis statement
Significance and broader impact of your work
How it fits within your field’s existing literature



2. Literature Review Strategy

Databases and search terms you will use
Type of review (systematic, narrative, scoping, etc.)
Timeline for completing your literature review



3. Methodology

Data collection methods (surveys, experiments, archival research, interviews, etc.)
Data analysis approach
Ethical considerations (IRB status if applicable)



4. Data Management

Types of data you will generate or collect
File organization and naming conventions
Storage, backup, and sharing plan
Data Management Plan (DMP) status



5. Citation and Reference Management

Citation management tool you are using
Citation style for your discipline



6. Publication and Dissemination Plan

Target journals or venues for publishing your research
Plans for sharing datasets or supplementary materials
Open access strategy
ORCID iD integration



7. Timeline

Key milestones (proposal defense, IRB approval, data collection, writing, submission)
Estimated completion dates

title: Module 7 (Capstone): Research Plan
text:
Writing Tips






TipYour Research Plan Is a Living Document



It will evolve as your research progresses. What matters most is showing that you’ve thought carefully about each component.



Be specific: Vague goals are hard to execute. “I will search PubMed and Scopus using the terms X, Y, Z” is better than “I will do a literature review.”
Be realistic: Your timeline should account for learning curves, advisor feedback, and unexpected delays.
Connect the parts: Show how your citation management, data management, and dissemination plans support each other.
Revise based on feedback: Share drafts with your advisor, subject librarian, and peers.

title: Module 7 (Capstone): Research Plan
text:
Writing Support Resources
Graduate Division has recordings to help with your writing:


Flexible Writing Practices (UCR credential required) Video
How to Write Concisely (UCR credential required) Video


Also refer to the Get Help section under each module for specific guidance on each section of your research plan.

title: Module 7 (Capstone): Research Plan
text:

Module 1: Citation Management — two assignments submitted on Canvas and review materials in the Resources of this website (also on Canvas)
Module 2: Scholarly Communication — two assignments submitted on Canvas and review materials in the Resources of this website (also on Canvas)
Module 3: Data Management — two assignments submitted on Canvas and review materials in the Resources of this website (also on Canvas)
Module 4: Literature Review Strategies — two assignments submitted on Canvas and review materials in the Resources of this website (also on Canvas)
Module 5: Publish & Share — quiz submitted on Canvas and review materials in the Resources of this website (also on Canvas)
Module 6: Graduate Academic Affairs — no assignment but review materials in the Resources of this website (also on Canvas)
Module 7 (Capstone): Research Plan — submit now!

title: Module 7 (Capstone): Research Plan
text:
Capstone Assignment






Important📋 Capstone: Research Plan



Submit a research plan (approximately 3–5 pages) addressing the components listed above. Your plan should demonstrate that you have engaged with the competencies covered in all six prior modules:

Citation management strategy (Module 1)
ORCID and scholarly communication plan (Module 2)
Data management plan summary (Module 3)
Literature review strategy (Module 4)
Publication and dissemination plan (Module 5)
Awareness of formatting and graduation requirements (Module 6)

Submit your research plan to your instructor on Canvas to receive your official certificate via Accredible.
Completion of all prior modules and assignments is required before submitting the capstone.

title: Module 5: Publish & Share Your Research
text:
After completing this module, you will be able to:

Understand who owns the copyright to your thesis or dissertation
Identify materials in your thesis/dissertation that may require permission
Apply fair use principles to digital projects and academic work
Understand Creative Commons licenses and choose the right one
Navigate the path from dissertation to book or journal publication

title: Module 5: Publish & Share Your Research
text:
Overview
This module covers copyright, licenses, fair use, and how to share and publish your thesis and dissertation with a publisher. It also introduces ProQuest submission, which is a graduation requirement covered in depth in Module 6.

✅ Complete the quiz assignment on Canvas to earn credit toward your certificate!

title: Module 5: Publish & Share Your Research
text:
Copyright Basics for Graduate Students

Who Owns Your Thesis or Dissertation?
In the U.S., you (the author) typically own the copyright to your thesis or dissertation. Copyright is established at the moment of creation.






Note



Important nuance: If a version of your dissertation is being published with a commercial publisher, the publisher agreement you sign may transfer some or all rights. Always read your publishing agreement carefully before signing.




What This Means for You

You can deposit your work in open access repositories (eScholarship, UC’s institutional repository)
You can post to your personal website
You must comply with any agreements you’ve signed with publishers for previously published chapters

title: Module 5: Publish & Share Your Research
text:
Materials That May Require Permission
When incorporating third-party content into your thesis or dissertation, some materials may require permission from the copyright holder:

📷 Photographs taken by others
🗺️ Maps from published sources
📊 Figures, tables, or graphs from journal articles or books
🎵 Music (lyrics, scores, recordings)
🎬 Film stills or video clips (including screenshots of Youtube videos)
📜 Lengthy quotations from books or articles

Even if something is on the internet or you own a copy of a book, this does not mean you have permission to reproduce it. Contact the copyright holder directly, or consult UCR Library.

title: Module 5: Publish & Share Your Research
text:
Fair Use
Fair use is a legal doctrine that allows limited use of copyrighted material without permission. Four factors are weighed:

Purpose — Educational/transformative use favored over commercial use
Nature — Factual works favored over creative works
Amount — Smaller portions favored over large portions
Market effect — Uses that don’t harm the market for the original are favored







Warning



Fair use determinations depend heavily on the specific facts of each case. When in doubt, seek permission or consult a librarian. UCR Library does not provide legal advice, but can help you think through the analysis.

title: Module 5: Publish & Share Your Research
text:
Creative Commons Licenses
If you want to make your work openly available, Creative Commons (CC) licenses let you specify exactly what others can do with it:








License
Code
Meaning




Attribution
CC BY
Others can use freely with credit


Attribution-ShareAlike
CC BY-SA
Must share under same license


Attribution-NonCommercial
CC BY-NC
No commercial use


Attribution-NoDerivatives
CC BY-ND
No modifications allowed


Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike
CC BY-NC-SA
NC + SA combined



Most open access funders (NIH and government funding agencies) require CC BY.

title: Module 5: Publish & Share Your Research
text:
From Dissertation to Publication
Turning your dissertation into a book or journal articles is an exciting but complex process.

Journal Articles

Identify target journals in your field
Check the journal’s submission requirements and copyright policy
Adapt your dissertation chapter to article format (typically 6,000–10,000 words)
Submit and revise through peer review



Book Publication (Review the presentation slides and recordings on From Dissertation to Book — Navigating the Publication Process in the Resources Section)

Identify academic presses in your field
Prepare a book proposal (overview, chapter summaries, market analysis)
Navigate the peer review and revision process
Review your publisher agreement carefully



ProQuest Submission
Submitting your thesis or dissertation to ProQuest is a graduation requirement — this is separate from transforming your work into a publication with a commercial publisher. See Module 6: Graduate Academic Affairs for the full submission process.

title: About This Certificate
text:
The Graduate Research Core Competencies Certificate is a self-paced online program developed by the UC Riverside Library to support graduate students at all stages of their research journey.


By completing this certificate, graduate students gain practical, transferable skills to:

Conduct, manage, and disseminate their research ethically and efficiently
Increase their research visibility and professional profile
Meet expectations of funding agencies and academic publishers
Navigate UCR’s graduation requirements with confidence




This certificate is designed for all UCR graduate students, regardless of discipline or career stage — whether you are just starting your program or preparing to file your dissertation.

To report issues or suggest improvements, please open an issue on GitHub.

title: Graduate Research Core Competencies Certificate
text:
UC Riverside Library · Self-Paced Certificate



Practical skills for every stage of your research journey — citation management, scholarly communication, data management, literature review, publishing, and more.
Complete all modules at your own pace to earn your certificate.

title: Graduate Research Core Competencies Certificate
text:
What You’ll Learn
The Graduate Research Core Competencies Certificate is designed to support graduate students at all stages of their research journey by building practical, transferable skills essential to scholarly work.
Participants develop strategies for:

Effective citation management using tools like Zotero and Paperpile
Advanced literature review techniques including systematic and scoping reviews
Responsible research data management and open data publishing
Scholarly communication — ORCID, researcher identity, and open access
Publishing & sharing theses, dissertations, and research articles
Graduation requirements — formatting, ProQuest, and Graduate Division workflows


The modules can be completed in any order, at your own pace. A certificate will be awarded upon completion of all modules.



  
    Submit Assignments on Canvas to Earn Points
    The knowledge checks on this website mirror the quizzes on Canvas and are for self-study practice. To receive credit toward your certificate, complete and submit all assignments through the Canvas course platform. Completing quizzes here does not count toward your grade.

title: Knowledge Checks
text:

title: Knowledge Checks
text:
Module 1: Citation Management Quiz



  2 questions
  



  1. Which of the following is a key advantage of Zotero over a manual bibliography?
   It automatically formats references in any citation style and syncs across devices
   It only works with Google Docs
   It requires a paid subscription for all features
   It can only store 10 references at a time
  
  Check Answer
  



  2. True or False: Paperpile is best suited for researchers who primarily write in Google Docs.
   True
   False
  
  Check Answer

title: Knowledge Checks
text:

  1. A researcher discovers that publications by someone with a similar name are appearing on their profile. What is the best next step?
  (Select all that apply)
   Ignore the issue
   Delete the entire profile
   Claim only verified works and use persistent identifiers
   Contact the journal editors
  
  Check Answer
  



  2. Your ORCID record should only include peer-reviewed articles.

  Check Answer

  Check Answer
  



  2. Fill in the blanks: While a [A] provides a high-level overview of a project and instructions on how to use the files, a [B] defines the specific variables, units, and labels used within the dataset itself.

title: Knowledge Checks
text:
Module 4: Literature Review Strategies Quiz



  3 questions
  



  1. Which type of review follows a rigorous, pre-registered protocol to minimize bias and produce reproducible results, often used to answer clinical or policy questions?
   Narrative review
   Systematic review
   Annotated bibliography
   Scoping review
  
  Check Answer
  



  2. When constructing a database search strategy, which technique involves combining synonyms and related terms to broaden your results?
   Using AND between terms
   Using OR between terms
   Using NOT between terms
   Using quotation marks around a phrase
  
  Check Answer
  



  3. True or False: An annotated bibliography includes both a citation and a brief descriptive or evaluative paragraph for each source.
   True
   False
  
  Check Answer

section: Module 5: Publish & Share Your Research Quiz
text:

title: Knowledge Checks
text:
Module 6: Graduate Academic Affairs Quiz



  3 questions
  



  1. Submitting your thesis or dissertation to ProQuest transfers your copyright to ProQuest.
   True
   False
  
  Check Answer
  



  2. Which of the following is a valid reason to request an embargo on your thesis or dissertation? (Select all that apply)
   A publisher has requested exclusivity for publication
   Your research contains sensitive data
   You want to keep your research private forever
   You are filing patents related to your work
  
  Check Answer
  



  3. When is the typical deadline for submitting your thesis or dissertation to the Graduate Division for degree conferral in a given quarter?
   The first day of the quarter
   The last day of final exams
   A specific filing deadline set by the Graduate Division, usually several weeks before the end of the quarter
   The day grades are due
  
  Check Answer
  












TipNote on Self-Paced Quizzes



These interactive questions are for self-study. For official certification, complete all module assignments on Canvas and submit the Capstone Research Plan on Canvas.

title: Module 3: Data Management
text:
After completing this module, you will be able to:

Explain what a Data Management Plan (DMP) is and why it is required
Use DMPTool to write a DMP for your research
Apply best practices for organizing, naming, and documenting data files
Understand the difference between open and proprietary file formats
Understand open data publishing with Dryad and increase your research impact

title: Module 3: Data Management
text:
Overview
This module introduces data management plans, an integral component of your research and data publishing. A DMP is required for IRB applications and grant applications. Publishing your data in an open access repository increases your publication count and supports data reuse and research validation. In addition to going through the summaries below, review the presentation slides, recordings, and other resources in the Resources Section.

✅ Complete the assignment at the end of this module and associated quiz on Canvas to earn credit toward your certificate!

title: Module 3: Data Management
section: What Is a Data Management Plan?
text:
What Is a Data Management Plan?
A Data Management Plan (DMP) is a formal document that describes:

What data you will collect or generate
How the data will be organized, stored, and backed up
What formats and standards you will use
Who will have access to the data during and after the project
How and where the data will be shared or preserved after the project ends







WarningWhen Are DMPs Required?




IRB (Institutional Review Board) applications at UCR
Federal grant applications (NSF, NIH, NEH, and most other funders)
Many journal and publisher submission requirements

title: Module 3: Data Management
text:
DMPTool
DMPTool is a free online tool that helps researchers write data management plans. It provides:

Funder-specific DMP templates (NSF, NIH, NEH, etc.)
Guidance text for each section
Collaboration features to share drafts with your advisor
Connection with the UCR Library to review your DMP and provide feedback
Example DMPs from other researchers

UCR Data Management Plan Guide — UCR Library’s guide to writing your DMP.

title: Module 3: Data Management
text:
Data Organization Best Practices

File Naming Conventions
Use consistent, descriptive file names that include:

Project identifier
Date (YYYY-MM-DD format)
Version number

Example: smith-dissertation-survey-data-2025-01-15-v2.csv


Folder Structure
Organize files into logical folders:
project/
  ├── raw-data/          # Never modify original data
  ├── processed-data/    # Cleaned, analysis-ready data
  ├── scripts/           # Analysis code
  ├── outputs/           # Figures, tables, reports
  └── docs/              # README, codebook, DMP


Documentation Files







Document
Purpose




README
High-level overview of the project and instructions for using the files


Codebook
Defines specific variables, units, and labels within the dataset


DMP
Overall data management strategy for the project lifecycle

title: Module 3: Data Management
text:
File Formats: Open vs. Proprietary
For long-term data sharing and archiving, open file formats are strongly preferred:



Data Type
Open Format (✅ Preferred)
Proprietary Format




Tabular data
.csv, .tsv
.xlsx (Excel)


Text
.txt, .md, .pdf/a
.docx (Word)


Images
.tiff, .png
.psd (Photoshop)


Audio
.wav, .flac
.mp3


Geospatial
.geojson, .shp
.mdb (ArcGIS)



Open formats can be read by any software now and in the future — proprietary formats may become unreadable if the software is discontinued.

title: Module 3: Data Management
text:
Data Publishing with Dryad
Dryad is a curated, open access data repository that makes research data freely available. Benefits include:

Assigns a DOI to your dataset — making it citable
Increases your citation count (data citations are recognized)
Facilitates reproducibility and research validation
Many journals require or recommend Dryad for supplementary data


Steps to Publish Data on Dryad

Organize and document your data (README + codebook)
Convert to open file formats where possible
Create a Dryad account (free for UCR affiliates)
Upload and describe your dataset
Receive a DOI and share it in your publications

text:
Resources

Slides: Data Publishing with Dryad — Dryad’s official documentation and guides Slides

Provide a written plan (one page) for your project’s data organization strategy, or submit a screenshot of an existing directory that demonstrates your organization. Your submission must show how you organize your files and where your README or Codebook is located.
Ensure your strategy is logical enough for a collaborator to navigate without your assistance.
Submission: Submit your plan to the Canvas course platform.
This assignment is required for the certificate.

title: Module 4: Literature Review Strategies
text:
After completing this module, you will be able to:

Distinguish between different types of literature reviews
Design an effective, reproducible search strategy
Use UC Library Search and discipline-specific databases effectively
Write an annotated bibliography
Select the most appropriate type of review for your research question

title: Module 4: Literature Review Strategies
text:
Overview
This module introduces the different types of reviews you may encounter in academic research. It guides you through designing an effective literature review search strategy and highlights UC Riverside Library resources that support you throughout the process. In addition to going through this page, review the tutorials and guides the at the Resources Section.

✅ Complete the assignment at the end of this module and the associated quiz on Canvas to earn credit toward your certificate!

title: Module 4: Literature Review Strategies
text:
Types of Reviews
Different research questions call for different review methodologies. Understanding which type fits your project is essential before you begin searching. The descriptions below are drawn from the Baylor University Evidence Synthesis guide on Types of Reviews.

Literature (Narrative) Review
A broad term referring to reviews with a wide scope and non-standardized methodology.

Search strategies, comprehensiveness, and time range covered vary and do not follow an established protocol.



Rapid Review
Applies systematic review methodology within a time-constrained setting.

Employs methodological “shortcuts” (limiting search terms, for example) at the risk of introducing bias.
Useful for addressing issues needing quick decisions.



Scoping Review or Systematic Map
Systematically and transparently collects and categorizes existing evidence on a broad topic or set of research questions.

Seeks to identify research gaps and opportunities for evidence synthesis.
May critically evaluate existing evidence, but does not attempt to synthesize the results in the way a systematic review would.
May take longer than a systematic review.



Umbrella Review
Reviews other systematic reviews on a topic.

Often defines a broader question than is typical of a traditional systematic review.
Most useful when there are competing interventions to consider.



Systematic Review
A methodical and comprehensive literature synthesis focused on a well-formulated research question.

Aims to identify and synthesize all of the scholarly research on a particular topic, including both published and unpublished studies.
Conducted in an unbiased, reproducible way to provide evidence for practice and policy-making and to identify gaps in research.
May involve a meta-analysis.
Much more time-intensive than traditional literature reviews.



Meta-Analysis
A statistical technique for combining the findings from disparate quantitative studies.

Uses statistical methods to objectively evaluate, synthesize, and summarize results.
May be conducted independently or as part of a systematic review.




Comparison at a Glance









Review Type
Scope
Methodology
Typical Use




Literature (Narrative) Review
Wide
Non-standardized
Background chapters, introductions


Rapid Review
Selective
Abbreviated systematic
Time-sensitive decisions


Scoping Review / Systematic Map
Broad
Systematic, no synthesis
Mapping gaps, feasibility


Umbrella Review
Broad
Reviews of reviews
Competing interventions


Systematic Review
Exhaustive
Rigorous, reproducible
Practice, policy, research gaps


Meta-Analysis
Selected studies
Statistical combination
Estimating effect sizes









Tip



Not sure which type fits your research? Consult your subject librarian or your advisor — they can help you match your research question to the appropriate methodology.

title: Module 4: Literature Review Strategies
text:
Designing Your Search Strategy
A good search strategy is systematic, reproducible, and documented. Follow these steps:

Step 1: Define Your Research Question
Use a framework like PICO (Population, Intervention, Comparison, Outcome) for clinical research, or PCC (Population, Concept, Context) for scoping reviews.


Step 2: Identify Key Concepts
Break your question into 2–4 core concepts. For each concept, brainstorm:

Synonyms and alternate spellings
Broader and narrower terms
Abbreviations and acronyms



Step 3: Choose Your Databases
Start with UC Library Search and Google Scholar (Google Scholar tends to include everything the good, bad and everythin in between, so better to use it critically)! You can also use databases appropriate to your discipline. Explore databases specific to your discipline that UCR Library has access to at the UCR Library A-Z Databases







Discipline
Recommended Databases




Sciences & Medicine
PubMed, Web of Science


Social Sciences and Humanities
JSTOR, Web of Science (Social Science Citation Index and Policy Citation Index), Academic Search Complete


Engineering
arXiv, IEEE Xplore


All disciplines
UC Library Search, Google Scholar





Step 4: Use Boolean Operators
Combine search terms with:

AND — narrows results (all terms must appear)
OR — broadens results (any term can appear)
NOT — excludes terms
Quotation marks — exact phrase search
Asterisk * — truncation (e.g., data* finds data, dataset, databases)



Step 5: Document Everything
Record every search: database, date, terms used, and number of results. This is required for systematic and scoping reviews, and good practice for any review.

title: Module 4: Literature Review Strategies
text:
Writing an Annotated Bibliography
An annotated bibliography combines a citation with a brief summary and evaluation of each source. It is useful for:

Tracking what you’ve read and why it’s relevant
Preparing for the literature review chapter of your thesis or dissertation
Demonstrating research depth in grant proposals


What Each Annotation Includes

Full citation (in your discipline’s style)
Summary of the work’s main argument or findings (2–3 sentences)
Evaluation of its relevance, methodology, or limitations (1–2 sentences)

title: Module 6: Graduate Academic Affairs
text:
After completing this module, you will be able to:

Understand UCR’s thesis and dissertation formatting requirements
Navigate the ProQuest electronic submission process
Identify key milestones and deadlines in the graduation timeline
Know which office to contact for graduation-related questions

title: Module 6: Graduate Academic Affairs
text:
Overview
If you are completing your thesis or dissertation at UC Riverside, you will first prepare your manuscript using the formatting guidelines provided by the Graduate Council. Before submitting, carefully review the requirements or attend a formatting workshop to ensure your document meets all university standards.
Once your committee has approved your final manuscript, submit it electronically via ProQuest for a final format review. After approval and all required forms are completed, your thesis or dissertation will be officially filed and you will be cleared for degree conferral.

title: Module 6: Graduate Academic Affairs
text:
Formatting Requirements
UCR Graduate Council sets specific formatting standards for all theses and dissertations. Key areas include:

Margins: Specific requirements for top, bottom, left, and right margins
Font and size: Approved fonts and minimum size requirements
Spacing: Line spacing requirements for body text, block quotes, and reference lists
Page numbering: Specific rules for preliminary pages vs. body pages
Title page: Required elements and exact formatting
Table of contents: Required formatting and depth







WarningAttend a Format Workshop Before You Submit



Formatting errors are one of the most common causes of delayed degree conferral. Graduate Division offers regular formatting workshops and detailed guides. Do not wait until the last minute.

title: Module 6: Graduate Academic Affairs
text:
The Submission Process

Step-by-Step

Draft and revise your thesis/dissertation with your advisor and committee
Attend a format workshop or review formatting guidelines carefully
Committee approval — get required signatures on all forms
ProQuest submission — upload your final PDF for format review
Format review — Graduate Division reviews and may request revisions
Final approval — once format-approved and forms completed, you are cleared for degree conferral
eScholarship deposit — your work is automatically deposited (unless you opt for an embargo)



Embargo Options
You may request an embargo (delay in public access) if:

A publisher has requested exclusivity for publication or you want to turn publish your dissertation work with a publisher in the future
Your research contains sensitive data
You are filing patents related to your work

Contact Graduate Division to discuss embargo options.

title: Module 6: Graduate Academic Affairs
text:
ProQuest and Your Rights
Submitting to ProQuest is a graduation requirement, but it is not the same as assigning all publishing rights. ProQuest’s default agreement:

Makes your work available in their dissertation database
Allows you to retain copyright
Does not prevent you from publishing with a commercial publisher

Always read the ProQuest publishing agreement before submitting. For questions, contact UCR Graduate Division.

title: Module 5: Publish & Share Your Research
text:
Copyright Decision Framework
When using third-party content or sharing your own work, work through following four steps. Review the presentation slides and recordings on Copyright & Fair Use for Digital Projects in the Resources Section to learn more.



Copyright decision framework: four steps for navigating permission, policy, and sharing.



Step 1: Do You Need Permission?
Ask three questions in sequence:

Is the work protected by copyright? Works in the public domain (old enough, U.S. government works, or CC-licensed) do not require permission.
Has a license already been granted? If the work carries a Creative Commons or open license, check its terms — you may already have permission.
Would your use be fair use? If yes to any of these questions, skip to Step 3. Otherwise, continue to Step 2.

Fair use is a legal doctrine allowing limited use of copyrighted material without permission. Four factors are weighed:

Purpose — Educational/transformative use favored over commercial use
Nature — Factual works favored over creative works
Amount — Smaller portions favored over large portions
Market effect — Uses that don’t harm the market for the original are favored







Warning



Fair use determinations depend heavily on the specific facts of each case. When in doubt, seek permission or consult a librarian. UCR Library does not provide legal advice, but can help you think through the analysis.




Step 2: Seek Permission if Needed
If permission is required:

Research who holds the copyright — it may be the author, publisher, or another rights holder — and send a written request.
Keep records of all correspondence and permissions granted.
Ensure the permission covers all intended uses — print and digital, all jurisdictions, etc.

Common materials in theses and dissertations that may require permission:

📷 Photographs taken by others
🗺️ Maps from published sources
📊 Figures, tables, or graphs from journal articles or books
🎵 Music (lyrics, scores, recordings)
🎬 Film stills or video clips (including screenshots of YouTube videos)
📜 Lengthy quotations from books or articles

Even if something is freely available online or you own a copy of a book, this does not mean you have permission to reproduce it.


Step 3: Address Non-Copyright Law & Policy Concerns
Even when copyright is not an issue, consider:

Contractual or terms of service restrictions from archives, databases, or websites that limit how their content may be used.
Privacy or publicity rights — concerns about identifiable individuals in your data or images.
Ethical norms — certain types of information (e.g., Indigenous cultural knowledge, sensitive research data) may carry obligations beyond copyright law.



Step 4: How Do You Want to Share?
Once copyright and policy concerns are resolved, decide how to make your work available:

Register your copyright — optional in the U.S., but registration provides legal advantages if infringement occurs. ProQuest offers registration at the time of dissertation submission.
License your work — Creative Commons licenses let you specify exactly what others can do with it (see below).
